title: The Feast of All Saints'
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
Sermon Date: November 6, 2022

## 1. LITURGICAL CONTEXT

This sermon was preached on the First Sunday after All Saints' Day (November 6, 2022), the day after the liturgical celebration of All Saints' Day (November 1). The RCL readings for this date would have been Revelation 7:9-17 (the great multitude in white robes), 1 John 3:1-3 (what we will be when Christ appears), and Matthew 5:1-12 (the Beatitudes). The church calendar was in Ordinary Time, following the extended period after Pentecost, with Advent beginning four weeks later on November 27.

## 2. EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was in the aftermath of General Convention 2022 (held in Baltimore in early July), which had approved updated canons on marriage and blessing of same-sex unions, a decision generating significant ongoing discussion within progressive and traditional parishes. The Diocese of Colorado, under Bishop Kym Harris (elected 2018), was navigating post-pandemic parish reopening and attendance patterns, with many urban Episcopal cathedrals experiencing mixed recovery. No major diocesan or national church controversies were at acute crisis point in early November 2022, though ongoing tensions over sexuality and theology remained steady background issues.

## 3. DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver experienced unusually warm fall weather through early November, with temperatures in the 70s Fahrenheit in the week before the sermon. Governor Jared Polis was in the final week of campaigning before the November 8 midterm elections, running strongly ahead of his Republican challenger; Colorado was solidly in the Democratic column for national races. The Cathedral's Capitol Hill neighborhood was relatively quiet in this period, though holiday season preparations were beginning at local businesses and community centers.

## 4. NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The dominant story was the November 8 midterm elections, now two days away, with polling showing stronger-than-expected Democratic performance, defying historical patterns of the party in power losing the House. Hurricane Ian had struck Florida as a Category 4 hurricane on September 28, but recovery efforts and political blame-assignment were still active news in early November. Elon Musk had completed his $44 billion acquisition of Twitter on October 27 and immediately began controversial mass layoffs and policy changes, generating daily headlines about the platform's direction. Inflation remained elevated at 8.2% (October CPI), and the Federal Reserve was widely expected to raise interest rates again at its November meeting, intensifying economic anxiety.

## 5. INTERNATIONAL NEWS

Russia's retreat from Kherson in southern Ukraine (completed by November 11, days after this sermon) was anticipated and being actively discussed; Ukrainian forces were pushing back against Russian positions after months of grinding conflict. The death of Queen Elizabeth II on September 8 had been followed by King Charles III's coronation preparations beginning, with Britain in formal mourning and transition. Iran was experiencing severe civil unrest following the September 16 death of Mahsa Amini in police custody, with ongoing protests and a heavy security response, generating international concern about human rights.

## 6. CULTURAL MOMENT

The national mood was tense and polarized, with election anxiety at peak intensity and a pervasive sense of crisis across multiple fronts (economic, geopolitical, institutional). The winter holiday season was beginning to appear in retail and media, providing some counterbalance to darker news cycles. A sermon on All Saints' Day—focused on communion with those who have died and the hope of resurrection—would have resonated with a congregation navigating apocalyptic headlines while also preparing for Thanksgiving and Advent, seeking spiritual grounding amid institutional uncertainty and national division.
